title: Lavender reed diffuser
subtitle: A DPG and lavender essential oil blend with rattan reeds
excerpt: A lavender reed diffuser made from lavender essential oil diluted in dipropylene glycol (DPG) carrier. DPG is the standard fragrance carrier for reed diffusers; very low odour, low viscosity, and wicks efficiently through rattan reeds without clogging. Eight reeds in a 100 ml bottle gives steady fragrance release for 6-8 weeks.
sourceNotes: Fragrance diffuser formulation notes, Bramble Berry (brambleberry.com, May 2026). DPG carrier properties and EO loading rates, Gracefruit fragrance-making guide (gracefruit.com, May 2026). Essential Oil Safety, Tisserand and Young (2014), lavender inhalation use.

recipe.batchNotes: Double to 200 ml for a larger bottle. Keep the ratio at 20% essential oil to 80% DPG.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Ready immediately after filling. The reeds take 1-2 hours to wick up the oil and begin releasing fragrance.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 8 months sealed (unused). Active in the diffuser, the blend lasts 6-8 weeks depending on the number of reeds, room temperature, and airflow. More reeds = stronger scent but faster evaporation. Flip the reeds every 3-4 days to refresh diffusion.

content.text: A lavender reed diffuser. Reed diffusers work by capillary action: the porous rattan reeds draw the fragrance blend up through their channels by the same mechanism that draws water up through plant stems. The carrier must be thin enough to wick efficiently; dipropylene glycol (DPG) is the standard because its viscosity and wetting properties allow even wicking along the full length of a 25 cm reed. Alcohol-based room sprays evaporate too quickly for sustained diffusion; carrier oils are too viscous to wick at all in a rattan reed.

Lavender essential oil at 20% gives a clear, sustained scent without being overpowering. Weaker blends (5-10%) produce an underwhelming result; stronger blends (above 30%) accelerate evaporation and shorten the active life. Position the diffuser away from direct sunlight and away from air vents, which accelerate evaporation. DPG is rated non-irritant to skin; avoid direct eye contact when handling the liquid.

items.prepNote: dipropylene glycol (DPG) fragrance carrier. 80% of the blend. The standard carrier for reed diffusers; low viscosity, low odour, efficient reed wicking.

Lavandula angustifolia lavender essential oil. 20% of the blend. Weigh for accuracy (approximately 18-19 g). A pure, high-quality lavender; synthetic fragrance oils also work but check they are diffuser-grade.
rattan diffuser reeds, 25-30 cm length. 8 reeds gives a moderate scent throw in a standard-sized room. Use fewer for a subtle background fragrance; more for a stronger effect.

content.text: Weigh the DPG into a small jug or beaker. Add the lavender essential oil and stir to combine. Pour through the small funnel into the amber glass bottle. Insert the rattan reeds. Within 1-2 hours the reeds will have drawn up enough blend to begin releasing fragrance. Flip all the reeds so the dry ends are now submerged; this jump-starts the diffusion.

Flip the reeds every 3-4 days; saturated reeds diffuse better than ones that have dried at the top. After 4-6 weeks the reeds may become clogged with dust and lose wicking efficiency; replace them. Top up the bottle with fresh blend if the level drops below 2 cm before the reeds are exhausted.

8 months sealed (without reeds), 6-8 weeks active in the diffuser. Store sealed bottles away from direct sunlight, which degrades the essential oil faster. DPG does not go rancid or expire; the limiting factor is the lavender EO, which oxidises over time and loses its clean floral character.
